ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for fried corn ribs; mapped to the nearest parent class 'deep-fried food product' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (sweet corn, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor; no specific code exists for deep-fried corn ribs with aioli. The aioli and frying oil components are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept found for fried corn ribs with assam aioli.

serving.natural_language_portion: One appetizer portion of fried corn ribs with assam aioli weighs approximately 250 g, typically served as a starter or sharing snack for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169998) and Mayonnaise, regular (FDC ID 172741); values weighted and adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for fried corn ribs with assam aioli. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for sweet corn (cooked) and regular mayonnaise, adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (approximately 8–10% fat uptake per standard frying literature). The input record values (energy 232 kcal, protein 3.4 g, fat 19.2 g, carbohydrate 12.0 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained. Sodium (260 mg/100g) reflects added salt and aioli contribution. All values are approximate; a laboratory analysis of the specific dish would be required for high-confidence figures.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; sweet corn analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried corn ribs with assam aioli. GI of 65 is estimated from sweet corn (GI ~60–70, mean ~65) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Deep-frying and fat from aioli may slightly lower the effective GI by slowing gastric emptying, but the base GI of sweet corn (65) is used as the conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 30.0 g) / 100 = 19.5, where available carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 12.0 × 2.5 = 30.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 260 mg/100 g; high total fat from deep-frying and mayonnaise-based aioli
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium load, request aioli on the side, and be aware that a 250 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, which represents roughly 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) from sweet corn base, high fat (19.2 g/100 g) impairing insulin sensitivity, low dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its medium glycaemic index, high fat content from deep-frying and mayonnaise aioli, and low fibre density; if consumed, a portion of no more than 100 g is advisable alongside a high-fibre accompaniment to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (19.2 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.12 g/100 g), and sodium (260 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and mayonnaise aioli
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its high total fat, saturated fat, and trans fat content from deep-frying and mayonnaise-based aioli, all of which contribute to atherogenic risk; the elevated sodium further increases cardiovascular strain and should be minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); chilli (nightshade) in aioli may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation, and the chilli component is a nightshade that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals; grilled corn with a tamarind-herb dressing is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Corn Ribs with Assam Aioli is a Singaporean fusion appetiser consisting of sweet corn cobs that are quartered lengthwise into rib-shaped pieces, seasoned with a homemade curry spice blend, and deep-fried until the kernels are crispy and caramelised on the outside while remaining tender within. The dish is served with a tangy assam (tamarind) aioli — a mayonnaise-based dipping sauce flavoured with tamarind paste, garlic, and chilli — and garnished with crispy fried curry leaves that add an aromatic, slightly bitter counterpoint. The flavour profile combines the natural sweetness of corn with the heat of curry spices, the sour-umami depth of tamarind, and the richness of the aioli. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense at approximately 232 kcal per 100 g, driven by a high fat content of 19.2 g/100 g from the frying oil and mayonnaise. Carbohydrates from sweet corn contribute 12.0 g/100 g with a medium glycaemic index of approximately 65. Sodium is elevated at 260 mg/100 g. The dish is not recommended for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high fat, moderate sodium, and medium-GI profile. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable. A standard appetiser portion is approximately 250 g, delivering around 580 kcal, 30 g carbohydrate, and 650 mg sodium per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried corn ribs?
Is corn ribs suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat fried corn ribs if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for sweet corn (cooked) and mayonnaise adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. The input record values are consistent with the composite estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from sweet corn data in the University of Sydney GI Database. A laboratory analysis of the specific dish would be required to achieve high confidence.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried corn ribs with assam aioli dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; the dish is a composite restaurant preparation with variable formulation. Input cultural tags (primary_cuisine: Italian, ethnic_tags: Italian/Pizza/Pasta) were incorrect and have been corrected to Singaporean fusion based on the dish description, ingredients, and country_of_origin field. The dish is a Singaporean café/fusion item popularised via food delivery platforms. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (65 × 30.0) / 100 = 19.5, using 250 g serving with 12.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
